The graphs of e^x (blue) and \ln(x) (orange) are reflections of each other
about the line y = x (dashed). The natural logarithm is only defined for
x > 0.

children.value:
The calculation rules for logarithms correspond directly to the calculation
rules for exponentials from the previous section. Each logarithm rule can be
derived from the corresponding exponential rule by applying

children.html: ln⁡\lnln

children.value:
to both sides
and using the identity

children.html: ln⁡(ex)=x\ln(e^x) = xln(ex)=x

children.value: The logarithm of a product equals the sum of the logarithms:

children.html: ln⁡(a⋅b)=ln⁡(a)+ln⁡(b).\ln(a \cdot b) = \ln(a) + \ln(b).ln(a⋅b)=ln(a)+ln(b).

children.value: This follows from the product rule for exponentials: if

children.html: a=ema = e^ma=em
b=enb = e^nb=en
a⋅b=em+na \cdot b = e^{m+n}a⋅b=em+n
ln⁡(a⋅b)=m+n=ln⁡(a)+ln⁡(b)\ln(a \cdot b) = m + n = \ln(a) + \ln(b)ln(a⋅b)=m+n=ln(a)+ln(b)

children.value:
The logarithm of a quotient equals the difference of the
logarithms:

children.html: ln⁡ ⁣(ab)=ln⁡(a)−ln⁡(b).\ln\!\left(\frac{a}{b}\right) = \ln(a) - \ln(b).ln(ba​)=ln(a)−ln(b).

children.value:
The logarithm of a power equals the exponent times the
logarithm:

children.html: ln⁡(an)=n⋅ln⁡(a).\ln(a^n) = n \cdot \ln(a).ln(an)=n⋅ln(a).

children.value:
This rule is particularly useful for solving exponential equations: it brings
the unknown exponent down from the power to a factor, where it can be isolated
by ordinary algebra.

Let us apply these rules to solve a general exponential equation. Suppose we
want to find

children.html: xxx
2x=102^x = 102x=10

children.value:
. Taking the natural logarithm of both
sides and applying the power rule:

children.html: x⋅ln⁡(2)=ln⁡(10),x \cdot \ln(2) = \ln(10),x⋅ln(2)=ln(10),
x=ln⁡(10)ln⁡(2)≈2.3030.693≈3.32.x = \frac{\ln(10)}{\ln(2)} \approx \frac{2.303}{0.693} \approx 3.32.x=ln(2)ln(10)​≈0.6932.303​≈3.32.
23.32≈102^{3.32} \approx 1023.32≈10

children.value:
. The same technique applies to any
equation of the form

children.html: ax=ba^x = bax=b
a,b>0a, b > 0a,b>0
a≠1a \neq 1a=1

children.value:
The natural logarithm is the standard choice in mathematics and engineering,
but two other logarithms appear often enough to deserve mention.

children.html: log⁡10(x)\log_{10}(x)log10​(x)

children.value:
uses base 10. It appears in the
definition of the decibel scale for sound and signal levels, in the pH scale
for acidity, and in the Richter scale for earthquake magnitudes. The common
logarithm is sometimes written simply as

children.html: log⁡(x)\log(x)log(x)

children.value:
without a base, especially
in engineering contexts.

children.html: log⁡2(x)\log_2(x)log2​(x)

children.value:
uses base 2. It appears in information
theory, where the amount of information in a message is measured in bits, and
in computer science, where algorithms are analyzed by how many times a problem
can be halved before it is solved.
Any logarithm can be converted to the natural logarithm using the

children.html: a>0a > 0a>0
a≠1a \neq 1a=1
log⁡a(x)=ln⁡(x)ln⁡(a).\log_a(x) = \frac{\ln(x)}{\ln(a)}.loga​(x)=ln(a)ln(x)​.

children.value: This formula follows directly from the definition: if

children.html: log⁡a(x)=y\log_a(x) = yloga​(x)=y
ay=xa^y = xay=x
yln⁡(a)=ln⁡(x)y \ln(a) = \ln(x)yln(a)=ln(x)
y=ln⁡(x)/ln⁡(a)y = \ln(x)/\ln(a)y=ln(x)/ln(a)

children.value:
. In practice
this means we only need one logarithm on a calculator: the natural logarithm
is sufficient to evaluate any other logarithm.

The natural logarithm is the inverse of the natural exponential function. Its
domain is

children.html: (0,∞)(0, \infty)(0,∞)

children.value: , it has a single zero at

children.html: x=1x = 1x=1

children.value:
, and it grows without
bound but increasingly slowly. The calculation rules for logarithms mirror those
for exponentials and are the main tool for solving equations where the unknown
appears in an exponent. Together,

children.html: exe^xex
ln⁡(x)\ln(x)ln(x)

children.value:
form a pair of inverse
functions that underpin an enormous range of engineering calculations. We now
turn to a completely different family of functions: the trigonometric functions,
which describe the oscillations and periodic phenomena that are equally
fundamental in mechanical engineering.
